fullText: we all know Enthropic has been struggling with one core issue in cloud code and that is memory consistency across your sessions that's exactly why a few months ago they introduced automemory a feature that gives Cloud the ability to take notes on your project and persist context over time but here's the issue after 20 plus sessions that memory starts to decay you end up with messy conflicting notes outdated debugging steps and vague timestamps like yesterday that completely lose meaning instead of helping the memory actually becomes noise and that's where hallucination starts to creep in now quietly without any official announcement Anthropic had just introduced a feature called /dream also known as autodream think of it like REM sleep for your AI agent it was found within their system prompt on GitHub and it's a feature that automatically consolidates Clots's memory between sessions it prunes stale or relevant notes versus useful insights and essentially cleans up the memory so it stays accurate and useful over time so instead of memory getting worse the more you use Claude it actually improves to use this new feature just simply open up your Claude instance and then type in /memory once you type this in you should see this new feature called autodream now obviously you're going to need to make sure you're on the latest update but to toggle it on just simply click enter and you're going to be able to now have it so that it is enabled and that you can use it directly behind your sessions now this isn't an official command that you can basically use to invoke it if you are to go back into your session if you type in /stream it's not going to work it will showcase unknown skill and this is because it's not an official command yet but if you are to do something like consolidate my memory using dream it is going to then use and invoke that dream feature so that it could potentially fully clean up the full process of your memory system it is going to be able to understand and run the full cleanup process using this new autodream feature and underneath the prompt bar you'll see that it is stating dreaming meaning that it has been turned on and another way to invoke it is just by simply typing in dream in your prompt autodream or just simply do what I did consolidate my memory which will trigger it manually by just simply using it without the /dream command which should be released soon if you want the best AI tools workflows and drops before everyone else join my free newsletter with the link in the description below which is completely free now that you have autodream turned on clot is basically running a background cleanup cycle on your entire memory system it goes through every memory file removes outdated information resolves contradictions and reorganizes everything so it actually makes sense but why is it called dreaming well this is because it's modeled exactly how our human brains work during our REM sleep during the day your brain is just collecting information everything you see do and learn but it can become quite messy and that's why it's your short-term memory then when you sleep your brain is replaying that information strengthens what matters deletes what doesn't and organizes it into long-term memory that's exactly what Autodream is doing automemory is Claude's daytime brain it just keeps taking notes every session autodream on the other hand is REM sleep it cleans organizes and strengthens those notes so they actually stay useful without the step Claude was basically sleepdeprived and it is just making messy notes over and over again under the hood Autodream runs in four key phases you first have orientation this is where Claude scans all existing memory files and builds a map of what it already knows then we have phase two and that is gather signal instead of reading everything it searches for high value signals like when you corrected it important decisions repeated patterns or anything you explicitly told it to remember from there we move on to phase three which is consolidation and this is the big step where it is actually cleaning things up it is something that will convert fake timestamps like yesterday into real dates it removes outdated or wrong information merges duplicate notes and updates old decisions like switching frameworks from there is the last step where it is going to prune and index it is going to now clean up the main memory index so it stays short relevant and actually usable at startup and this is how it is functioning with this four-step process and something that you want to know is that autodream doesn't run consistently it only triggers at least 24 hours after it has passed and that you have more than five sessions this is going to prevent unnecessary processing that keeps active projects clean so just keep that in mind and if you're looking to get the system prompt for it you can take a look at it in the description below which I'll leave for this documentation something that I'll also want to state is that it can't touch your code only memory files it runs in the background so you won't actually notice it and it uses a lock system so multiple instances don't break your memory now you may wonder when should you actually use it well this is something that you can manually trigger like how I just did by stating and invoking Dream in my prompt and you can essentially trigger it when you did a a big refactor when you changed a framework or tool for example if you're moving from Express to Fastify you can essentially use this feature to have it invoke the new memory system you can also use it when you had a lot of trial and error debugging or when things start to feel inconsistent or that when your coding session is starting to fall off basically anytime your project is evolving this is when you want to use the dream feature and what you'll actually notice is that once you run Dream it is going to stop repeating outdated solutions it is going to have fewer hallucinations and more consistent answers across sessions overall this will enable better understanding of your project decision which is a gamecher so essentially you see that I was able to use the dream command to basically clean up my memory and claude basically did this by understanding the new project it was able to create a structured memory file for it which you see over here and it even recalled previous memories in this case it updated my global index as well which is great where it specifically created an MD for this project it captured my full stack it was also able to link it to my memory system which is huge because it doesn't just store notes it understood my full architecture so in this case if I was to normally just work on a project or just work on multiple projects odd would start mixing up a lot of these things which is why it tends to elucidate a lot and it's not able to keep clear context of whatever you're working upon if you have multiple instances in this case it's able to keep a fair logic on everything that I'm doing within this project where it's going to get a better understanding of the APIs the logic and even the framework that I'm using in essence Dream turns the project into reusable knowledge before Claude was kind of like a stateless helper it is something that I would have to start with from scratch every time I ran a new session but now Claude builds a mental model per project it keeps it clean every single time and that is why it is a gamecher this feature should be coming out fairly soon i would expect it to come out probably next week early next week because they are releasing a lot of auto features like auto mode which just came out today so I would expect it to come out probably early next week if you like this video and would love to support the channel you can consider donating to my channel through
